problem_summary: 2011 MCM Problem B (discrete/network optimization): find the MINIMUM number of VHF repeaters needed to support 1,000 simultaneous low-power users in a flat 40-mile-radius circular area, given that repeaters must be >=10 miles apart, broadcast +/-600 kHz from the received signal across a 3 MHz band (145-148 MHz), and that 54 CTCSS (PL) subaudible tones are available to multiply channel capacity. Extensions: scale to 10,000 users and discuss line-of-sight degradation in mountainous terrain. The team reframed this as a 2D facility-location / coverage problem driven by a known geographic user distribution.
models_used: k-means cluster analysis on user (x,y) coordinates to find dense user centroids and 'score' each cluster by the number of users a single repeater placed there would cover (coverage = users within separation distance d_s)

Model 1 - the 'Bender' Snake Model: a greedy constructive/iterative heuristic that grows a single chain ('snake') of open repeaters. First open repeater placed at the user with the most neighbors within d_s; each subsequent open repeater placed a fixed output distance d_h away in the direction of the highest-scoring cluster centroid (Algorithm 2 sweeps the candidate at each cluster angle and picks the one covering the most uncovered users). Spirals/snakes around the map until N_f >= 1000.

Model 2 - the Branching Model: builds a 'backbone' of open repeaters along the line between the two highest-scoring clusters (Algorithm 3 steps repeaters d_h apart until distance s < d_h), then RECLUSTERS after every iteration and connects the next highest-scoring cluster to the nearest existing repeater via a shortest-path branch, creating a tree rather than a loop.

CTCSS (Private Line) channel-capacity sub-model: tracks a running channel-deficit D = max(N_c - O, 0) where O = 3/Delta_f channels are added per open or CTCSS repeater; when D>0 a CTCSS repeater is placed via a 360-direction angular sweep (Algorithm 1) to cover the most deficit users; in the Branching Model some CTCSS tones are reserved as dedicated long-distance chains.

Line-of-sight propagation model: effective range (miles) = sqrt(1.5 * A_f), where A_f is antenna height in feet; used to tie repeater output distance d_h to tower height h_r (e.g. d_h=15 mi -> 150 ft tower) and to argue the height/cost tradeoff.

Parity (frequency up/down, +/-600 kHz) assignment model: post-processing graph 2-coloring of the repeater network so no repeater connects to the rest only through same-parity neighbors, preventing a signal from being repeatedly stepped up/down out of band.
key_techniques: Reframing a frequency-coordination problem as a 2D geometric coverage/facility-location problem driven by the user spatial distribution
Greedy constructive heuristics with an explicit per-step objective ('place where the most currently-uncovered users are covered') rather than a global MILP
Angular sweep search: discretizing direction into a partition of [0,360] (|P|=360) and brute-forcing the best placement angle at fixed radius d_h or d_p

Reclustering (re-running k-means on only the still-uncovered users after each placement) so the heuristic always targets the current highest-priority demand - explicitly required in the Branching Model

Running two contrasting models (snake/loop vs backbone/tree) as an internal cross-check, then comparing repeater counts to argue the 'minimum' is credible and to expose the connectivity-vs-count tradeoff

Decoupling the two scarce resources: physical coverage (open repeaters, governed by d_s/d_h) handled first, channel capacity (CTCSS lines, governed by deficit D and Delta_f) handled as a separate accounting layer
Repurposing CTCSS tones for a second job - dedicated long-distance backbone lines - beyond their nominal interference-reduction role
Generating synthetic but realistic test instances (a centered-city/suburb distribution and an 8-small-town rural distribution) to validate the model on qualitatively different geographies
Stress-testing by feeding ~12,000 users and lowering Delta_f from 25 kHz to 10 kHz to show graceful scaling to the 10,000-user case
Sensitivity analysis on k (5/10/20), separation distance d_h (10/15/20 mi, mapped to tower height & cost), and initial population (1400/3000/10000) presented in compact tables
why_it_won: "It converted an unfamiliar amateur-radio domain into a clean, well-posed geometric optimization and then delivered an actual concrete number (17 repeaters minimum for the city case, 18 for rural) with a defensible algorithm behind it - judges reward a crisp answer to the literal question ('minimum number'). The two-model strategy is the standout move: rather than claiming one heuristic is optimal, the team built a second, structurally different model (snake-loop vs backbone-tree) and used the agreement/gap between them (17 vs 26; 18 vs 25) both as a sanity check on the minimum and as a vehicle to articulate the deeper insight that 'better connectivity costs more repeaters.' The technology grounding is thorough and cited (the 10-mile spacing rule, the sqrt(1.5*height) line-of-sight law, the 600 kHz parity constraint, the 54 CTCSS tones), so every modeling choice traces to a real constraint. The work handles the channel-capacity dimension that weaker teams ignore entirely - separating coverage from channel deficit and using CTCSS as a distinct resource. Finally it closes every loop the prompt opened: 10,000 users (42 repeaters with Delta_f dropped to 10 kHz), mountainous terrain, plus real sensitivity analysis on k, separation distance, and population - demonstrating the model is robust, not a one-off."
section_highlights: "Strong structural choices: (1) A dedicated 'Available Technology' section up front that explains repeaters and CTCSS and pins down every numeric constraint before any modeling - this educates the judge and justifies later assumptions. (2) Each model gets a Description + Mathematical Interpretation pair plus a labeled flowchart (Figures 1 & 2) and boxed pseudocode (Algorithms 1-3), so a reader can grasp the idea visually then verify it formally - excellent dual-channel exposition. (3) A clear parameter table per model (n, k, d_s, h_r, d_h, Delta_f, l_n, l_c) that doubles as the interface for the sensitivity analysis. (4) An explicit 'Model Comparison Summary' enumerating similarities and differences before the case studies - it tells the judge exactly what to watch for. (5) Two contrasting case studies (city vs rural) each carried through identically: placement narrative, repeater plot, parity diagram, count. (6) Honest, specific Strengths & Weaknesses (names QT/Quality-Threshold clustering as an untried alternative, admits the 'circling to hunt the last user' failure). (7) The abstract states both models and previews the stress tests, and the conclusion delivers numbered takeaways. Reproducibility is reinforced by an appendix of full-page plots and full source code."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The 'minimum' is from a greedy/constructive heuristic, never proven optimal - no lower bound, no MILP/exact baseline, no comparison to a packing bound, so '17 is the minimum' is an empirical claim from two heuristics that happen to agree-ish, not a guarantee

Sole reliance on k-means; the paper itself flags that Quality-Threshold clustering was never tried and that exclusive k-means limits the result. Results also depend on k-means random initialization, which is not addressed (no averaging over seeds)

A printed distance formula has a sign error: the Euclidean distance is written with a minus sign between the squared terms ((dx)^2 - (dy)^2) instead of a plus - a typo that, if taken literally, is not a valid metric
Test instances are self-generated synthetic distributions, not the real or worst-case geographies; the model is validated on cases the team chose, which can flatter the heuristic

The mountainous-terrain section is purely qualitative narrative - no quantitative LOS/terrain model, no diffraction or path-loss computation, just a discussion of placing repeaters on peaks vs routing around ranges

The 10,000-user 'scaling' partly comes from shrinking channel width Delta_f (25->10 kHz) and from the assumption that potential users greatly exceed the target, which inflates density and makes coverage easier - the favorable population-vs-repeater table (1400->10000 users needing fewer open repeaters) is a direct consequence of that assumption
Odd-length repeater loops break clean parity 2-coloring (acknowledged 'signal leakage'); the fix is hand-waved as 'the signal travels the other way around the loop'

Several modeling assumptions are strong and only lightly defended: isotropic antennas, every user identical/low-power, perfectly known user distribution, no outside interference, no cost objective beyond a qualitative tower-height note
reusable_patterns: Two-model cross-validation: when a prompt asks for a 'minimum/optimal' value with no exact method available, build two structurally different heuristics and use their agreement to argue credibility and their gap to surface a tradeoff insight - far stronger than one tuned heuristic

Cluster-then-place pipeline for any facility-location / coverage problem: k-means to find demand centroids, score centroids by coverage, greedily place facilities, then RECLUSTER on remaining demand each iteration to chase the current hotspot

Angular/grid sweep as a cheap optimizer: discretize a placement direction or position (e.g. partition [0,360] into 360 angles) and brute-force the best candidate - simple, transparent, easy to defend to judges

Resource decoupling: separate two coupled scarce resources into independent accounting layers (here physical coverage vs channel capacity, tracked by an explicit running deficit D) so each can be reasoned about and optimized cleanly

Constraint-traceability: open with a 'Available Technology / Background' section that converts every domain rule (spacing, propagation law, band limits) into a named parameter, so every later modeling decision cites a constraint
Dual exposition of every algorithm: pair a plain-English description + flowchart with boxed formal pseudocode and a parameter table - lets judges both understand and verify
Stress test by parameter, not just by size: scale the instance AND show which knob you turned to absorb the stress (here lowering Delta_f for 10x users), making the scaling mechanism explicit
Sensitivity tables that map an abstract parameter to a real-world cost (d_h -> tower height in feet -> cost), turning a math knob into an engineering decision the client can own

Honest self-critique that names specific alternatives you didn't try (e.g. QT clustering) and a concrete failure mode (heuristic 'circling to find the last user'), which reads as competence rather than weakness
Generate qualitatively distinct synthetic test cases (dense-center vs many-small-clusters) to demonstrate the method generalizes across the realistic input regimes
